"All the wild-fowl sang them to him,
"In the vale of Tawasentha,
By the rushing in the Spring-time,
By the black line in the Winter;
Flap like eagles in their eyries;--
To this Song of Hiawatha!
That the feeble hands and helpless,
Groping blindly in the darkness,
Touch God's right hand in that darkness
Through the green lanes of the country,
Where the tangled barberry-bushes
On a half-effaced inscription,
Moulded it into a pipe-head,
Bending like a wand of willow,
Waving like a hand that beckons,
Painted like the sky of morning,
Looked upon their wrath and wrangling
Listen to the words of wisdom,
I have given you brant and beaver,
Weary of your wars and bloodshed,
Therefore be at peace henceforward,
And as brothers live together.
If his warnings pass unheeded,
You will fade away and perish!
Then upon the ground the warriors
Washed the war-paint from their faces.
Soiled and stained with streaks of crimson,
Cried the warriors, cried the old men,
On the summit of the mountains,
Spotted brown and gray with mosses.
Till the hot breath of his nostrils
Had you conquered me in battle
Like a cowardly old woman!"
When one goes to fish in Winter.
Thus was slain the Mishe-Mokwa,
He the terror of the nations.
Gave the others to his children;
With the brightest streaks of crimson,
Till into a star he changed her,
When the Wawa has departed,
Felt his icy breath upon him,
Wrestled all night with the North-Wind,
Hearing still the gusty laughter,
Shawondasee, fat and lazy,
And the grapes in purple clusters.
Till one morning, looking northward,
"Ah! my brother from the North-land,
Thus the Four Winds were divided
Had their stations in the heavens,
Hiawatha's Childhood
In the unremembered ages,
"See! a star falls!" said the people;
"From the sky a star is falling!"
There among the ferns and mosses,
There among the prairie lilies,
With the beauty of the moonlight,
Rose the black and gloomy pine-trees,
Bright before it beat the water,
Bedded soft in moss and rushes,
Running straight across the heavens,
Lighting up the brakes and bushes,
Whispered, "What is that, Nokomis?"
When on earth they fade and perish,
And the birds sang round him, o'er him,
And a deer came down the pathway,
By the ford across the river;
As he bore the red deer homeward,

"All the wild-fowl sang them to him,
Saying as he left each wigwam,
"In the vale of Tawasentha,
By the rushing in the Spring-time,
By the black line in the Winter;

That the feeble hands and helpless,
Groping blindly in the darkness,
Through the green lanes of the country,
Where the tangled barberry-bushes
On a half-effaced inscription,
Of the tender pathos
Moulded it into a pipe-head,
The Peace-Pipe that rises from it!
Said the great Gitche Manito,
Bending like a wand of willow,
Waving like a hand that beckons,
Painted like the sky of morning,
Looked upon their wrath and wrangling
Listen to the words of wisdom,

The Peace-Pipe, ghastly with war-paint,
Cried aloud the old men,
Spotted brown and gray with mosses.
Till the hot breath of his nostrils
When one goes to fish in Winter.
Thus was slain the Mishe-Mokwa,
He the terror of the nations.
Call him Mudjekeewis no longer,
Gave the others to his children;
With the brightest streaks of crimson,
Till into a star he changed her,
Wabun gazing at the maiden,
He it was whose name was Wabun,
Wabasco, the rabbit, leaping,
When the Wawa has departed,
Singing all his songs in chorus,
Wrestled all night with the North-Wind,
Hearing still the gusty laughter,
Singing, "You are but my fellow-mortal!"
Shawondasee, fat and lazy,
And the grapes in purple clusters.
Till one morning, looking northward,
"Ah! my brother from the North-land,
Thus the Four Winds were divided;
In the unremembered ages,
"See! a star falls!" said the people;
"From the sky a star is falling!"
Waub-Nokomis, Nokomis!
By the shores of Gitche Gumee,
Rose the black and gloomy pine-trees,
Bright before it beat the water,
Bedded soft in moss and rushes,
Running straight across the heavens,
Lighting up the brakes and bushes,
By the ford across the river;
As he bore the red deer homeward,
And when of age he became,
He could shoot ten arrows upward,
Shoot them with such strength and swiftness,
That the tenth had left the bow-string
Ere the first to earth had fallen!
He had mittens, Minjekahwun,
Made of deer-skin, soft and pliant,
He could walk a mile without them,
On the crusted snow and icy,
Leaving not a trace behind him.
He had moccasins enchanted,
Made of deer-skin, soft and supple,
He could walk a mile in these
On the crusted snow and icy,
Leaving not a trace behind him.
He could shoot ten arrows upward,
Shoot them with such strength and swiftness,
That the tenth had left the bow-string
Ere the first to earth had fallen!
Much he questioned old Nokomis
Of his father Mudjekeewis;
Learned from her the fatal secret
Of the beauty of his mother,
Of the falsehood of his father;
And his heart was hot within him,
Like a living coal his heart was.
Then he said to old Nokomis,
"I will go to Mudjekeewis,
See how fares it with my father,
At the doorways of the West-Wind,
At the portals of the Sunset!"

gold: With loss of EDEN, till one greater Man
Fast by the Oracle of God; I thence
Stird up with Envy and Revenge, deceiv'd
If he oppos'd; and with ambitious aim
Reserv'd him to more wrath; for now the thought
Such place Eternal Justice had prepar'd
Cloth'd with transcendent brightnes didst outshine
Myriads though bright: If he whom mutual league,
United thoughts and counsels, equal hope,
And to the fierce contention brought along
That durst dislike his reign, and me preferring,
Doubted his Empire, that were low indeed,
That were an ignominy and shame beneath
Fearless, endanger'd Heav'ns perpetual King;
And put to proof his high Supremacy,
Whether upheld by strength, or Chance, or Fate,
Then such could hav orepow'rd such force as ours)
Have left us this our spirit and strength intire

gold: With loss of Eden, till one greater Man
That shepherd who first taught the chosen seed
Invoke thy aid to my adventurous song,
Say first--for Heaven hides nothing from thy view,
Had cast him out from Heaven, with all his host
In adamantine chains and penal fire,
Torments him: round he throws his baleful eyes,
At once, as far as Angels ken, he views
A dungeon horrible, on all sides round,
And rest can never dwell, hope never comes
For those rebellious; here their prison ordained
Oh how unlike the place from whence they fell!
Clothed with transcendent brightness, didst outshine
And high disdain from sense of injured merit,
That with the Mightiest raised me to contend,
With suppliant knee, and deify his power
Who now triumphs, and in th' excess of joy
Fearless, endangered Heaven's perpetual King,
Whether upheld by strength, or chance, or fate,
Than such could have o'erpowered such force as ours)
But see! the angry Victor hath recalled
The fiery surge that from the precipice
If not, what resolution from despair."
Heap on himself damnation, while he sought
With solid, as the lake with liquid fire,
Sublimed with mineral fury, aid the winds,
That we must change for Heaven?--this mournful gloom
What matter where, if I be still the same,
We shall be free; th' Almighty hath not built
Here for his envy, will not drive us hence:
And call them not to share with us their part
Which, but th' Omnipotent, none could have foiled!
Of that inflamed sea he stood, and called
While with perfidious hatred they pursued
Warriors, the Flower of Heaven--once yours; now lost,
Your wearied virtue, for the ease you find
To adore the Conqueror, who now beholds
Yet to their General's voice they soon obeyed
Poured never from her frozen loins to pass
The heads and leaders thither haste where stood
Of mankind they corrupted to forsake
Came singly where he stood on the bare strand,
Among the nations round, and durst abide
From Aroar to Nebo and the wild
To do him wanton rites, which cost them woe.
Not tried or manacled with joint or limb,
Can execute their airy purposes,
And works of love or enmity fulfil.
To whose bright image nightly by the moon
While smooth Adonis from his native rock
In his own temple, on the grunsel-edge,
For one of Syrian mode, whereon to burn
Or altar smoked; yet who more oft than he
His own and Rhea's son, like measure found;
Downcast and damp; yet such wherein appeared
Semblance of worth, not substance, gently raised
With gems and golden lustre rich emblazed,
Of flutes and soft recorders--such as raised
Moved on in silence to soft pipes that charmed
Of dreadful length and dazzling arms, in guise
Awaiting what command their mighty Chief
In fable or romance of Uther's son,
Waiting revenge.  Cruel his eye, but cast
For ever now to have their lot in pain--
With singed top their stately growth, though bare,
Self-raised, and repossess their native seat?
For me, be witness all the host of Heaven,
Henceforth his might we know, and know our own,
Should favour equal to the Sons of Heaven.
For who can think submission? War, then, war
Belched fire and rolling smoke; the rest entire
A numerous brigade hastened: as when bands
Or cast a rampart.  Mammon led them on--
That riches grow in Hell; that soil may best
Of Babel, and the works of Memphian kings,
With wondrous art founded the massy ore,
Within, her ample spaces o'er the smooth
As from a sky.  The hasty multitude
Where sceptred Angels held their residence,
Erring; for he with this rebellious rout
At Pandemonium, the high capital
In spring-time, when the Sun with Taurus rides.
Fly to and fro, or on the smoothed plank,
Thus incorporeal Spirits to smallest forms
Celestial Virtues rising will appear
Hath been achieved of merit--yet this loss,
Of present pain that with ambitious mind
More than can be in Heaven, we now return

gold: Through all temptation, and the Tempter foiled
Now had the great Proclaimer, with a voice
With awe the regions round, and with them came
His witness unconfirmed: on him baptized
That heard the Adversary, who, roving still
About the world, at that assembly famed
Flies to his place, nor rests, but in mid air
"O ancient Powers of Air and this wide World
(For much more willingly I mention Air,
How many ages, as the years of men,
In manner at our will the affairs of Earth,
Must bide the stroke of that long-threatened wound
For this ill news I bring: The Woman's Seed,
Destined to this, is late of woman born.
Invites, and in the consecrated stream
And out of Heaven the sovraign voice I heard,
But must with something sudden be opposed
He ended, and his words impression left
For long indulgence to their fears or grief:
In Adam's overthrow, and led their march
Regents, and potentates, and kings, yea gods,
Where he might likeliest find this new-declared,
Temptation and all guile on him to try--
But, contrary, unweeting he fulfilled
They now, and men hereafter--may discern
So spake the Eternal Father, and all Heaven
And, devilish machinations, come to nought!"
Meanwhile the Son of God, who yet some days
Lodged in Bethabara, where John baptized,
Of Saviour to mankind, and which way first
Not wilfully misdoing, but unware
O Son! but nourish them, and let them soar
To what highth sacred virtue and true worth
Before Messiah, and his way prepare!
Straight knew me, and with loudest voice proclaimed
Accompanied of things past and to come
Under the covert of some ancient oak
The lion and fierce tiger glared aloof.
Where aught we hear, and curious are to hear,
Whom thus answered the Arch-Fiend, now undisguised:--
That he might fall in Ramoth, they demurring,
Or virtuous; I should so have lost all sense.
Imparts to thee no happiness, no joy--
Yet thou pretend'st to truth! all oracles
Which they who asked have seldom understood,
And, not well understood, as good not known?
In every province, who, themselves disdaining
Then to thyself ascrib'st the truth foretold.
The Gentiles; henceforth oracles are ceased,
In pious hearts, an inward oracle
Smooth on the tongue discoursed, pleasing to the ear,
To whom our Saviour, with unaltered brow:--
"Thy coming hither, though I know thy scope,
The desert; fowls in their clay nests were couched;
Sought lost Eliah, so in each place these
Nigh to Bethabara--in Jericho
Send thy Messiah forth; the time is come.
By his great Prophet pointed at and shown
Lay on his providence; He will not fail,
To have conceived of God, or that salute,
And fears as eminent above the lot
I lost him, but so found as well I saw
Had left him vacant, and with speed was gone
Each of his reign allotted, rightlier called
Consenting in full frequence was impowered,
Far other labour to be undergone
However to this Man inferior far--
Therefore I am returned, lest confidence
Among daughters of men the fairest found.
Many are in each region passing fair
As the noon sky, more like to goddesses
Draw out with credulous desire, and lead

gold: Love has gone and left me and the days are all alike;
And all I saw from where I stood
Was three long mountains and a wood.
The sky, I thought, is not so grand;
I 'most could touch it with my hand!
I screamed to feel it touch the sky.
The creaking of the tented sky,
The ticking of Eternity.
Lay open to my probing sense
That stood behind each envious thrust,
About a thousand people crawl;
Perished with each,--then mourned for all!
I felt his gaze, I heard his moan,
Between two ships that struck and sank;
No hurt I did not feel, no death
That, crying, met an answering cry
Beating against my lips I heard;
Yet lay the weight so close about
Full six feet under ground did lie,
I lay and heard each pattering hoof
For soon the shower will be done,
Shakes joyously, and each round drop
And let the heavy rain, down-poured
I ceased; and through the breathless hush
Laid gently on my sealed sight,
A drenched and dripping apple-tree,
And yet it seems to me, if I should speak,
How like reality the dream had been;
That book, outspread, just as you laid it down!
Perhaps you thought, "I wonder what comes next,
So rose, and left it, thinking to return.
In this brown book I gave you. Here your hand,
You would have dropped your pen and come to me
There is a dignity some might not see
The things that withered,--and you came not back!
Had let us love,--and show the world the way!
When love-crossed-out will bring the answer right!
Of ten small words a rope to hang the world!
In trivial expression, that have been
Summer?  'Tis summer still by the calendar!
Set back the world a little turn or two!
Your golden filaments in fair design
Aches in my breast; part of my heart lies chilled
In the damp earth with you.  I have been torn
A Fear that in the deep night starts awake
Dark, Dark, is all I find for metaphor;
Is down, and all opposed things flow together
Into a vast monotony, where night
That clutter up the world?  You were my song!
Now, let discord scream!  You were my flower!
Plant things above your grave--(the common balm
I cannot even turn my face this way
Would tangle in the frantic hands of God
Ah, I am worn out--I am wearied out--
And now I go.  Nor threat, nor easy vow
Lonely I came, and I depart alone,
Is mine to bear away of that old grace
If thou hadst left my little joys alone!
Thus I to Life, and ceased, and slightly smiled,
Whereof Life held content the useless key,
So stood longtime, till over me at last
Leading me back unto my old abode,
On the unlovely garb in which I came;
I wandered through the house.  My own, my own,
I know not when the wonder came to me
Wondering, I sat, and watched them out of sight.
And the next day I called; and on the third
Chamber wherein he for so many years
That grows to naught,--I love thee more than they
God's World
Thy mists, that roll and rise!
To crush!  To lift the lean of that black bluff!
I will touch a hundred flowers
Up from the town,
People dress and go to town;
All my thoughts are slow and brown:
Of all the grey-eyed people
Aye, 'tis a curious fancy--
This or that or what you will is all the same to me;
There's this little street and this little house.
I knew her for a little ghost
Her gown's white folds among.
And where the wall is built in new
And weeds were tall on all the paths that led that way!
In a dress dark as my hair
White and awful the moonlight reached
I said,--for Love was laggard, O, Love was slow to come,--
I lay,--for Love was laggard, O, he came not until dawn,--
